coredata.dc:title: Enhanced pyrophosphate detection: Utilizing oPD-derived carbon dots and Fe3+ interactions in a paper strip biosensor
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Garg, Rishabh
Prasad, Dinesh
coredata.dc:description: The development of portable, cost-effective, and straightforward DNA biosensors holds immense importance in various fields, including healthcare, environmental monitoring, and food safety. This study contributes to the objective by introducing an innovative approach for synthesizing carbon dots (Cdots) with high quantum yield (QY) and remarkable selectivity for Fe3+ ions. Utilizing o-phenylenediamine as a precursor, the study achieved a straightforward and environmentally friendly synthesis method, enabling the efficient detachment of metal ions from the Cdot surface upon introducing pyrophosphate (PPi). The presence of surface hydroxyl and amino groups facilitated specific Fe3+ recognition. Employing D-optimal response surface methodology, the study optimized Cdot synthesis parameters, identifying temperature and heating time as critical factors influencing QY. Statistical analysis confirmed the model's reliability, predicting maximum QY of 48.8 % with minimal deviation from experimental results. Characterization studies revealed the amorphous nature of Cdots through HR-TEM, XRD, and FTIR analysis. Furthermore, the proposed LAMP/PPi biosensing technique demonstrated higher sensitivity, specificity, and repeatability, with negligible interference from common anions and efficacy across varying pH levels. The limit of detection (LOD) of 0.079 (±0.01) μM and the detection range of 0.1 μM–2 mM underscore the biosensor's practical utility. This study highlights a promising direction for developing paper-based LAMP/PPi biosensors with potential diagnostics and environmental monitoring applications. Significantly, the biosensing technique is applicable to any DNA amplification method generating pyrophosphate (PPi) as a by-product.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Portable DNA biosensors
Quantum yield optimization
Pyrophosphate detection
LAMP biosensing
Food safety monitoring

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental design, factor optimization and synthesis
Paper based biosensor for LAMP/PPi detection
Cdots synthesis, D-optimal optimization and statistical analysis
The Cdot/Fe3+ probe: quenching and restoration of fluorescence
Sensitivity, specificity and repeatability of the biosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Dinesh Prasad reports financial support was provided by Birla Institute of Technology. DINESH PRASAD reports a relationship with Birla Institute of Technology.The authors thank Dr Joydeep Dhar, Sophisticated Instrumentation Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, the Central Instrumentation Facility, BIT Mesra, and Material Research Center, MNIT Rajasthan, for the technical and instrumentation support. RG received Institute Research Scholarship from the parent institute (APO/PhD/IRS/2023-24/61). DP gratefully acknowledges the SMS grant (GO/Estb/SMS/2019-20/2122) from BIT Mesra.

The authors thank Dr Joydeep Dhar, Sophisticated Instrumentation Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, the Central Instrumentation Facility, BIT Mesra, and Material Research Center, MNIT Rajasthan, for the technical and instrumentation support. RG received Institute Research Scholarship from the parent institute (APO/PhD/IRS/2023-24/61). DP gratefully acknowledges the SMS grant (GO/Estb/SMS/2019-20/2122) from BIT Mesra.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of: (A) sample collection, DNA amplification through LAMP and its sensing via Cdot/Fe3+ Paper Strip in Various Samples; (B) Fluorescence restoration in carbon dots via competitive Fe3+ displacement by pyrophosphate.

MODDE-generated plots for D-Optimal design optimization in oPD-Carbon dots synthesis (A) Contour plot illustrating the effects of parameters on the quantum yield; (B) Summary of fit plot; (C) Observed versus predicted plot.

Cdots characterization (A) FT-IR spectra; (B) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra; (C) HR-Tem images; (D) Fluorescence Decay curve of the prepared Cdots and Fe3+ passivated Cdots under 420 nm excitation.

(A) Comparison of the influence of various cations on Cdots fluorescence; (B) Stern-Volmer plot depicting Cdots + Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 0.1 mM to 1 mM; (C) Determination of the Stern-Volmer constant (KSV = 3.24 × 103 M−1) at lower concentrations of Fe3+ in Cdot solution; (D) Visual representation of Cdot/Fe3+ complexes undergoing a noticeable colour transition from yellow to red with increasing Fe3+ ion concentration in aqueous solution. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Agarose gel electrophoresis image showing Lane 1- 100bp Ladder, lanes 2-4- LAMP amplicons; (B) Visual representation of color change and fluorescence restoration of Cdot/Fe3+ probe solution at different concentrations of PPi, C1- Cdots/Fe3+ probe, C2–C11 increasing concentrations of PPi in probe solution, C12 pure Cdot aqueous solution; (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of the probe at different concentrations of PPi; (D) Standard plot of fluorescence intensity versus PPi concentration. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Paper strip based biosensing of LAMP byproduct pyrophosphate, as seen in visible light and ultraviolet light.
Graphical representation illustrating (A) the effects of common anions; (B) Repeatability test of the biosensor.
Experimental design of synthesis of Cdots from o-Phenylenediamine.
Experimental runs and observed vs. predicted quantum yields from MODDE software.
ANOVA for D-Optimal quadratic model in quantum yield optimization.

head.ce:title: Enhanced pyrophosphate detection: Utilizing oPD-derived carbon dots and Fe3+ interactions in a paper strip biosensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bioengineering and Biotechnology, Birla Institute of Technology, Mesra, Jharkhand, 835215, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bioengineering and Biotechnology, Birla Institute of Technology, Mesra, Jharkhand, 835215, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of portable, cost-effective, and straightforward DNA biosensors holds immense importance in various fields, including healthcare, environmental monitoring, and food safety. This study contributes to the objective by introducing an innovative approach for synthesizing carbon dots (Cdots) with high quantum yield (QY) and remarkable selectivity for Fe3+ ions. Utilizing o-phenylenediamine as a precursor, the study achieved a straightforward and environmentally friendly synthesis method, enabling the efficient detachment of metal ions from the Cdot surface upon introducing pyrophosphate (PPi). The presence of surface hydroxyl and amino groups facilitated specific Fe3+ recognition. Employing D-optimal response surface methodology, the study optimized Cdot synthesis parameters, identifying temperature and heating time as critical factors influencing QY. Statistical analysis confirmed the model's reliability, predicting maximum QY of 48.8 % with minimal deviation from experimental results. Characterization studies revealed the amorphous nature of Cdots through HR-TEM, XRD, and FTIR analysis. Furthermore, the proposed LAMP/PPi biosensing technique demonstrated higher sensitivity, specificity, and repeatability, with negligible interference from common anions and efficacy across varying pH levels. The limit of detection (LOD) of 0.079 (±0.01) μM and the detection range of 0.1 μM–2 mM underscore the biosensor's practical utility. This study highlights a promising direction for developing paper-based LAMP/PPi biosensors with potential diagnostics and environmental monitoring applications. Significantly, the biosensing technique is applicable to any DNA amplification method generating pyrophosphate (PPi) as a by-product.

ce:section.ce:para: In the midst of the global COVID-19 pandemic, the critical role of DNA biosensors has been underscored as an indispensable component of diagnostic strategies. The demand for rapid and accurate detection has fueled the need for advanced molecular diagnostic tools. DNA biosensors have become key in addressing these urgent requirements [1]. Despite the strides made in DNA biosensor technology, certain gaps persist, impeding their seamless integration into diagnostic workflows. Challenges include the need for improved sensitivity, specificity, and multiplexing capabilities [2,3]. Additionally, enhancing the robustness of biosensors for use in resource-limited settings remains a crucial objective.

Carbon dots (Cdots) are increasingly recognized as potent materials for maximizing sensing and detection. They are gaining prominence in DNA biosensors due to their unique properties [4,5]. With exceptional optical features, including strong fluorescence and high photostability, Cdots serve as ideal signal transducers [6]. Their biocompatibility ensures minimal interference with biological samples, while easy surface functionalization allows for the customization of biosensors for specific applications [7]. Factors like precursor concentrations, reaction time, temperature, and pH influence quantum yield and Cdot synthesis [8,9]. Response Surface Methodology (RSM) efficiently navigates this, minimizing runs and offering a thorough understanding of interactions and quadratic effects [10,11]. A comprehensive study on influencing factors is needed, making RSM integration crucial for systematic optimization.

Numerous DNA biosensors using Cdots exist, but their integration and operation are intricate. A more straightforward method for DNA detection has emerged through pyrophosphate (PPi) sensing, employing Cdots [12,13]. PPi is a by-product of various physiological and biochemical processes, is generated during DNA amplification when phosphodiester bonds form between nucleotides [14]. Although RT-PCR and PCR methods remain the gold standards for genetic diagnosis, they come with inherent benefits and limitations. In contrast, the Loop-Mediated Isothermal Amplification (LAMP) is a much simpler amplification technique that operates at a constant temperature and requires minimal machinery. LAMP produces a substantial amount of PPi, which can be measured using the on-and-off fluorescence mechanism of Cdots', offering a simple and direct approach to DNA biosensing [15,16]. They engage with metal ions, forming probes that are widely employed for PPi sensing in diverse applications, including cellular imaging, urine tests, and disease diagnostics [17]. Their heightened sensitivity to metal ions enables the detection of metallic species in varied samples, creating fluorescence turn-off or on probes [18,19]. Cdots' fluorescence is quenched by metal ions (e.g., Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Al3+) [20], leading to stable Cdot-metal ion complexes. This quenching involves phenomena such as electron exchange, Static Quenching Effect (SQE), Inner Filler Effect (IFE), Aggregate-Induced Emission Quenching (AIEQ), and Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) [19].

The Cdot-metal ion probe is versatile, suitable for use in sensing strategies such as genetic material detection and capable of offering diverse result outputs including colorimetric, fluorometric, electrochemical, and electro-chemiluminescent. Turn-off detection relies on the selective coordination-like interactions between Cdot surface functional groups (carboxylate, hydroxyl, and amino) and metal ions [20,21]. The fluorescence turn-on detection of PPi is achieved through dissociating the Cdot-metal ion complex, driven by the preferential complexation of PPi with the metal ion [22,23]. Numerous studies have explored the use of Cdot probes, primarily for the simultaneous detection of Fe3+ alongside other metal ions and PPi in aqueous solutions, particularly for environmental or intracellular imaging applications. However, research into DNA biosensing using this probe remains limited. There is a notable absence of convenient, affordable, portable paper-based DNA amplification sensing strips for on-site diagnosis. Existing paper-based DNA sensors often rely on methods such as wax printing or costly modifications like gold nanoparticles, hindering widespread implementation and industrial acceptance. This work presents a straightforward approach to on-site genetic-based sensing of microbes or pathogens, potentially addressing this gap in the field.

The study aimed to synthesize bright yellow-emitting Cdots through a one-step RSM-supported hydrothermal method using a single source, and to utilize the synthesized Cdots for PPi detection in DNA amplification products, with regained fluorescence intensity directly correlating with PPi concentration. The study outcomes were colorimetric and fluorometric, and the technique was miniaturized onto a simple paper-based portable strip biosensor, as shown in Fig. 1 A and B. With potential industrial modifications, this method could become a compact paper-based DNA biosensor for point-of-care (POC) use, applicable to various sample types. It shows great promise for advancing DNA biosensors.

Tris-(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (TRIS), glacial acetic acid, Ethylenedinitrilotetraacetic acid disodium salt dihydrate (EDTA), Iron(III) chloride anhydrous and o-Phenylenediamine (99.5 %), all of analytical reagent grade, were procured from Merck, Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). The DNA dye SYBR® Green I was obtained from CarlRoth GmbH + Co. KG (Germany). The components of the PCR Master Mix and Invitrogen SYBR™ Safe DNA Gel Stain were imported from Thermo Fisher Scientific™ (USA), and the Bst DNA Polymerase, Large Fragment was imported from New England Biolabs (USA). All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (resistivity 18.2 MΩ cm) from the Milli-Q® EQ 7000 Ultrapure Water Purification System (Germany). The primers were acquired from Barcode Biosciences (India).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental design, factor optimization and synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Response Surface Methodology (RSM) was applied using a D-optimal quadratic design to synthesize Cdots with high QY using the MODDE® 13 software (Sartorius Stedim Data Analytics AB, Sweden) [24]. Table 1 displays the low (−1), medium (0), and high (+1) factor levels for each design parameter involved in creating the randomized sequence of the factors: oPD concentration (X1), reaction temperature (X2), and heating time (X3). The synthesis mixtures, formulated using software-generated experiment sets, involved dissolving oPD in ultrapure water and heating it in a customized hydrothermal autoclave reactor lined with polypropiolactone. After cooling, the crude Cdots solutions were diluted in ultrapure water and filtered through cellulose filter paper. The resulting filtrates were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to further purify, and the collected supernatant was reserved for subsequent use. This study comprised 13 experimental runs, with QY as the response factor, expressed as a percentage, as indicated in Table 2 .

The impact of synthesis conditions specifically, oPD concentration, reaction temperature, and heating time on QY was statistically assessed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), with a p-value less than 0.05 as statistically significant for all experimental data. The model was fitted using multiple linear regression (MLR) analysis at a 95 % confidence interval [25].

The Lambda-25 UV–Visible Spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) was used to investigate optical absorption, while the Frontier FTIR Spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA) was utilized to obtain the FTIR spectra. The Cdots' size and morphology were examined using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) (FEI Tecnai G2 F20, USA) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Raman spectroscopy measurements were carried out using a confocal Raman microscope (inVia™, Renishaw, UK). XRD patterns were obtained using a Rigaku Smart Lab 9 KW X-Ray Diffractometer (Japan) equipped with Cu-Kα radiation. Zeta potential measurement were conducted using the Nano ZS Particle Size Analyzer (Malvern Inst., UK). Recording of fluorescence spectra and quantum lifetime was performed using the Shimadzu RF 5301 PC Spectrofluorophotometer (Japan) and FLS1000-SS-stm Photoluminescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd, UK), respectively.

The fluorescence QY of all prepared Cdots was measured at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm, represented by Eq. (1).
(1)
Q Y
s
=
Q Y
r
(
m s
m r
)
(
η s 2
η r 2
)
Here, ‘s’ represents the Cdots, subscript ‘r’ refers to Rhodamine B, ‘QY’ denotes the Quantum Yield, ‘m’ represents the slope of the standard curve of the QY, and ‘η’ indicates the refractive index of the solvent. Rhodamine B and Cdots were dissolved in ultrapure water, and the absorbance was maintained below 0.1 to avoid the reabsorption [26]. Rhodamine B served as the standard with 31 % QY.

For carbon dot-metal ion (Cdot/MI) based probe development, 1 mM solutions of common metal ions (Fe3+, Na+, K+, Al3+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Hg2+) were prepared, and 40 μL of Cdots were added to each solution. Subsequently, the metal ion that exhibited the most pronounced quenching effect on the fluorescence of Cdots, coupled with an observable alteration in the colour of the Cdot solution in distilled water, was identified. Thereafter, The Cdots' surface was passivated with Fe3+ ions by blending the Cdot solution with varying concentrations of FeCl3 (ranging from 0.1 mM to 5 mM), enabling the development of a probe to detect PPi. The fluorescence spectrum was acquired with an excitation wavelength set at 420 nm, and both excitation and emission slits widths were fixed at 5 nm. A fluorescence assay targeting PPi was executed, and the probe's sensitivity for PPi detection was assessed. The mechanism underlying the quenching phenomenon was elucidated by applying the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (2)).
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
s v
[ Q ]
Here, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the Cdots in absence and presence of the quencher (Fe3+), KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant and Q is the concentration of the quencher. All experiments and measurements were done at room temperature in ultrapure water for simplicity and minimal reliance on specialized buffers.

A pure culture of Salmonella typhimurium (MTCC 98) was obtained from the Microbial Type Culture Collection and Gene Bank (Chandigarh, India). Genomic DNA isolation was carried out using the Qiagen QIAamp® DNA Minikit. The LAMP reaction consisted of a 25 μL master mix with 1.6 μM each of FIP and BIP, 0.2 μM each of F3 and B3, 0.4 μM of each LF and LB, Bst DNA polymerase (320 U/mL), 1.4 mM dNTP mixture, 8 mM MgSO4, and isolated DNA as the template in a 1X isothermal buffer mix. The primers targeted the conserved Salmonella enterotoxin (stn) region, a standard feature in all Salmonella sp [27,28] (Table S1). The LAMP reaction occurred at 65 °C for 60 min in MiniAmp™ Plus Thermal Cycler (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Confirmation of the LAMP reaction was achieved through 2 % agarose gel electrophoresis and visualization using a gel imaging system (VILBER FUSION SOLO S, France). Fluorometric and colorimetric detection of the LAMP reaction was accomplished by adding the final LAMP product (2 μL–10 μL) to the aqueous dispersion of the Cdots/Fe3+ probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper based biosensor for LAMP/PPi detection
ce:section.ce:para: General-purpose cellulose filter paper (Cytiva, United States) was cut into dimensions of 3.0 cm × 1.0 cm. The paper strips were then immersed in 2 mL of the probe solution, comprising oPD-derived Cdots (6 μg/mL) titrated with a 2 mM Fe3+ solution for 10 min with gentle shaking. The strips were dried at room temperature for 30 min. After drying, the strips were tested by applying LAMP products and various anions separately on different strips. The resulting fluorescence changes were observed by illuminating the strips under a UV torch (EX = 420 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cdots synthesis, D-optimal optimization and statistical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize high QY with enhanced selectivity of Cdots for Fe3+ ions, o-phenylenediamine a straightforward, non-toxic organic precursor with a loose binding affinity for Fe3+ was employed [29]. This precursor allowed Fe3+ ions to detach readily from the Cdot surface upon introduction of PPi into the solution, thus facilitating their binding to PPi molecules and establishing the foundation for the LAMP biosensing technique mentioned herein. The abundant surface hydroxyl and amino groups on the Cdots are deemed essential for the specific recognition of Fe3+. Thus, the application of D-optimal response surface methodology was crucial for developing high QY Cdots. This methodology enabled systematic experimentation to examine the impact of various factors on QY. The model indicated that the combination of temperature and heating time significantly influenced Cdots formation, with coefficients of 35.71 and 7.02, respectively (Fig. 2 A, Fig. S1). Conversely, elevated concentrations of the source material (oPD) may negatively impact Cdots formation, as indicated by a coefficient of −3.69 concerning QY. As shown in Table 2, the maximum QY (48.8 %) was observed in Run 8 with only 0.01 % deviation from the predicted values at 0.2 g oPD, a reaction temperature of 200 °C, and heating time of 8 h. This was further utilized in subsequent experimental procedures detailed in this work.

The Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) results in Table 3 , derived from the D-optimal quadratic model, demonstrate statistical significance with a p-value of 0.00, meeting the reference criterion (p ≤ 0.05). The model shows no lack of fit, validated by a p-value of 0.244, which exceeds the reference threshold of p > 0.05 [30]. Additionally, MODDE software generated a summarized fit plot that facilitate the interpretation of the model via multiple linear regression analysis. The model exhibits a high level of fitness, evidenced by an R2 value of 0.999 indicating a close alignment with the dataset [31]. Moreover, the experiment demonstrates exceptional predictive capability, with a Q2 value of 0.996, well above the threshold of 0.5 for good model predictability. Assessment of Model validity and reproducibility recorded at 0.99 and 0.64, respectively, confirms the model's reliability and validity(Fig. 2B). This robust relationship between experimental data and the empirical model underscores its practical utility in predicting optimal responses. The strong correlation between actual and predicted values affirms the model's adequacy. Additionally, the normal distribution with equal variance in the residuals vs. predicted QY plot reinforces the suitability of employing D-optimal for modelling Cdots synthesis, as shown in Fig. 2C.

The FTIR spectrum of Cdots displays a distinctive broad band ranging from 3100 to 3600 cm−1, indicating the presence of amino (N–H) and oxygen-containing (O–H) functional groups on their surfaces (Fig. 3 A). Vibration bands with wavenumbers from 1300 to 1000 cm−1 are attributed to stretching C–O and C–N bonds. Prominent bands at wavenumbers 1575, 1634, and 1730 cm−1 suggest the presence of aromatic CC, the carboxylic acid (CO, O–H), and amide stretching frequencies [32]. The hydrophilic nature of Cdots is attributed to the presence of hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on their surface. These functional groups contribute to the remarkable aqueous solubility and hydrophilicity of Cdots. Furthermore, the confirmation of yellow emission suggests a close association with the presence of surface oxygen-containing species [22]. Optical characterization reveals absorption spectra with peaks around 300 nm, indicative of π−π* transitions within the Cdot's core structure. Additionally, a peak around 420 nm corresponds to n−π* edge transitions arising from surface functional groups (Fig. S2). The maximum emission (EM) occurs at 550 nm with an excitation wavelength (EX) of 420 nm (Fig. 3B). The fluorescence lifetime (τ0) of Cdots is determined to be 1.85 ns (average of τ1 and τ2).

HR-TEM analysis reveals that Cdots predominantly form agglomerates, with most particles smaller than 10 nm (Fig. 3C). These particles exhibit no discernible crystalline fringes, confirming the material's amorphous nature, as supported by selected area electron diffraction (SAED) [33]. Raman spectroscopy provides an intensity ratio (ID/IG) of 1.04, indicating disordered graphite lattice vibrations suggestive of defects or amorphous carbon [34,35]. XRD analysis of Cdots shows a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 20°, consistent with the (002) crystal planes of graphene, indicating the presence of amorphous carbon attributed to the turbostratic carbon phase, signifying imperfectly arranged graphite crystals positioned in random directions [36,37]. The zeta potential measurement at pH 7 reveals a surface charge of −12.84 mV for Cdots. Results of absorbance spectra, SAED and XRD (Fig. S2) are attached in the Electronic Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Cdot/Fe3+ probe: quenching and restoration of fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4A illustrates the influence of different cations on the fluorescence of Cdots in ultrapure water with Fe3+ showing the most significant quenching effect. Consequently, ferric iron was chosen for further study in this research. Dissociative Fe3+ coordinates with surface functional groups to generate aggregates, leading to fluorescence quenching. The Stern-Volmer plot in Fig. 4 B displayed a linear trend at lower Fe3+ concentrations (0.1 mM–1 mM) and shifted to a curve and exponential quenching at concentrations exceeding 1 mM, indicating a complex quenching mechanism. Dynamic quenching occurs at lower Fe3+ concentrations, where Fe3+ and Cdots formed a reversible complex during collisions [38], with the degree of quenching is being proportional to the quencher concentration, resulting in a linear relationship. The Stern-Volmer constant (KSV = 3.24 × 103 M−1) was determined by calculating the slope of the linear graph at lower concentrations (Fig. 4C). The graph's linearity at higher concentrations was indicated by plotting the square of the quencher concentration [Q2] in the modified Stern-Volmer equation. At higher Fe3+ concentrations (exceeding 1 mM), the curve suggested a transition to static quenching, forming stable, non-fluorescent complexes. The excess concentration of Fe3+ may result in the saturation of available binding sites on the Cdots. This excess can create non-fluorescent complexes, contributing to the observed curve and exponential quenching in the Stern-Volmer graphical representation. Consequently, the time-resolved fluorescence analysis exposed a reduction in Cdots lifetime to 0.92 ns in the presence of Fe3+ (Fig. 3D). This reduction is attributed to potential electron transfer to Fe3+'s d-orbitals, activating the nonradiative electron−hole recombination pathway [39,40]. These findings highlight the dynamic impact of Fe3+ on Cdot fluorescence behavior. Additionally, in the aqueous state, Cdot/Fe3+ complexes exhibit a visible colour change from yellow to red as Fe3+ ion concentration increases (Fig. 4D). This provides a reliable colorimetric output that correlates to the added Fe3+ amount, ensuring accurate results.

The practical feasibility of the suggested assay was confirmed through fluorometric detection of the LAMP reaction. LAMP is a molecular biology technique, amplify DNA under isothermal conditions. Unlike traditional polymerase chain reaction (PCR), which requires temperature cycling, LAMP operates at a constant temperature of about 60–65 °C [41]. The reaction involves strand displacement DNA synthesis, leading to the accumulation of substantial DNA quantities, accompanied by the generation of elevated levels of PPi as a byproduct. Exceeding a threshold of 0.5 mM, the PPi forms precipitates in the form of Mg-PPi molecules, causing turbidity in the post-LAMP product solution [42]. For this study, gDNA of S. typhimurium (Fig. S3), a prevalent foodborne pathogen, served as the DNA template for LAMP. Successful LAMP amplification was confirmed by agarose gel electrophoresis [43], as shown in Fig. 5 A. Subsequently, the post-LAMP solution was employed for sensing purposes using the proposed technique.

The gradual introduction of LAMP products containing PPi led to the detachment of Fe3+ ions from Cdots, forming iron pyrophosphate and resulting in a proportional increase in fluorescence. This process indicated a displacement or competitive binding mechanism, where PPi competed with Fe3+ binding to Cdots, effectively displaying the metal ion. These displaced Fe3+ ions then formed a non-fluorescent complex, which allowed for the recovery of fluorescence. This regained fluorescence directly correlated with the amount of PPi introduced, with higher concentrations leading to greater fluorescence. The standard plot shows the linearity of the established relation between regained fluorescence intensity and PPi concentration (Fig. 5C and D). It served as the basis for a successful turn-on and turn-off fluorescence Cdot/metal ion probe, particularly useful in DNA amplification detection where PPi is generated as a byproduct. In addition to fluorescence recovery, a visible color change from red back to yellow was noted in the probe solution, with the extent of color restoration directly proportional to the PPi concentration(Fig. 5B). While previously reported probes operate effectively at microconcentrations, this probe is engineered to remain stable at higher concentrations (1–2 mM). Enabling visible results via naked-eye in colorimetric tests. Moreover, simply exposing the sample to a UV torch allows for easy observation of fluorescence restoration. This enhances the applicability of the sensing strips for broader and on-site rapid testing devoid of sophisticated instrumentation.

Equivalent tests were conducted on the Cdot/Fe3+ probe-coated biosensing strips by introducing 10–15 μL of the LAMP product solution. A noticeable color shift from red to yellow was observed, with the restoration of fluorescence visibly under UV light (Fig. 6 ). Quantification of the fluorescence recovery on the strip was performed using the fluorometer (Prizm Fluoro Mod, Testright, India). This work was closely related to paper microfluidics, a rapidly advancing field that utilizes the capillary action of paper to manipulate fluids for various analytical and diagnostic purposes [44]. The capillary action of the paper facilitated the movement of the LAMP products across the strip, allowing for rapid and efficient detection. The microfluidics mechanism in this work relies on the wicking properties of cellulose filter paper, which spontaneously directs fluid flow along its channels due to capillary action [45]. Upon application of the PPi-containing LAMP products to the sample region of the paper strip, capillary action drew them through the channels. As the fluids progressed, they encountered carbon dots coated with Fe3+ions. The interaction with PPi initiated a chemical reaction, reactivating the fluorescence of the carbon dots within the strip's yellow zone. This reaction allows for rapid and sensitive detection in a paper-based microfluidic platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity, specificity and repeatability of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The probe solution and the strip biosensor remain unaffected by the interference of common mentioned anions (Fig. 7 A), demonstrating no impact from DNA strands or other LAMP master mix, thus confirming their specificity. The limit of detection (LOD) calculated was found to be as low as 0.079 ± 0.01 μM depicting the probe's sensitivity and the detection range was found to be 0.1 μM to 2 mM for PPi. The results from the nano-biosensing probe were compared to those obtained using the Merck Pyrophosphate Assay Kit (Sigma-Aldrich, USA), which utilizes a fluorogenic sensor operating at EX/EM of 316/456 nm. The detection data were comparable to those obtained with the kit, showing minimal differences (Fig. S4). While the kit has a sensitivity limit of ≥0.1–0.4 μM, this probe detected concentrations below 0.1 μM. Additionally, commercial kits are expensive and require low temperatures for storage. In contrast, the strips and probes work across various temperatures, require minimal maintenance, and are cost-effective and straightforward. The strips show normal detection in temperature range of 20–60 °C. The repeatability tests were done by repeating the detection tests 15 times and taking the observations (n = 3) against a positive blank (FPC), the regained fluorescence from the set of experiments was divided by the fluorescence regained in positive blank of Cdot/Fe3+ probe with 0.5 mM PPi, as shown in Fig. 7B. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated out to be 1.4 %.

The synthesis of Cdots with high QY and enhanced selectivity for Fe3+ ions was successfully demonstrated. Utilizing (oPD) as a precursor enabled a straightforward and non-toxic synthesis approach. This method facilitated the efficient detachment of metal ions from the Cdot surface upon introduction of PPi, thus establishing the foundation for developing a LAMP biosensing technique. Additionally, employing D-optimal response surface methodology allowed for the optimization of Cdot synthesis parameters, revealing the significant influence of temperature and heating time on QY. Statistical analysis confirmed the model's significance and reliability, with high fitness, predictability, validity, and reproducibility. The proposed LAMP biosensing technique demonstrated good sensitivity, specificity, and repeatability for PPi detection, with negligible interference from common anions. The paper-based platform for the probe to create sensing strips can have potential applications in various fields, including diagnostics and environmental monitoring. The biosensors can rapidly and accurately detect genetic material (DNA or RNA amplification), enabling timely diagnosis of genetic diseases, monitoring microbial contamination in water and food supplies, and identifying pathogens in clinical samples. The portability allows for on-site testing, reducing the need for sample transportation and enabling real-time monitoring in remote or resource-limited settings. Moreover, their cost-effectiveness makes them accessible to a broader range of users, including small clinics, research laboratories, and field workers. Overall, the availability of such biosensors plays a crucial role in enhancing public health, environmental protection, and food security.
This research did not involve human or animal samples.
The datasets generated during and/or analysed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

Rishabh Garg: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Dinesh Prasad: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:
Dinesh Prasad reports financial support was provided by Birla Institute of Technology. DINESH PRASAD reports a relationship with Birla Institute of Technology.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Dr Joydeep Dhar, Sophisticated Instrumentation Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, the Central Instrumentation Facility, BIT Mesra, and Material Research Center, MNIT Rajasthan, for the technical and instrumentation support. RG received Institute Research Scholarship from the parent institute (APO/PhD/IRS/2023-24/61). DP gratefully acknowledges the SMS grant (GO/Estb/SMS/2019-20/2122) from BIT Mesra.

ce:section.ce:para:
